• The world isn't beautiful or rosy. We trick ourselves to think it's beautiful to live on.
  • Can machines question their own existensialism? If a script is written to explicitly question their consciousness, is it really conscious? In preparation for the Turing test questions such as, "Are you human?", if a machine is explictly programmed to answer intelligently, in the eyes of the arbiter, how is this not a pass? Humans are deterministic exactly like machines. We just happen to be made of cells instead of plastic, and neurons have larger memory capacity than any man-made chips/storage so far.
  • If we are part of a simulation a la Nick Bostrom, is there a Turing test version for humans that is analogous to man-made machines? What sort or a level of intelligence test are we supposed to test in the eyes of the simulator (creation of the simulation)?
  • If humans can code explictly for machine to fail the Turing test, which is easily achieveable without any effort with the current state of artificial intelligence, it's possible humans are failing some version of Turing test at the moment in the simulation world. It's possible to hard-code some machine so that it doesn't ask certain questions, thus intentionally failing certain tests. Similar argument could be made on humans in the simulation world. Humans may have been hard-wired to evade certain existential questions or to fail higher-dimensional Turing test, all in the context of simulation/simulator.
